name: How is a sliding glass door properly waterproofed so it doesn’t leak later?
headline: How is a sliding glass door properly waterproofed so it doesn’t leak later?

mainEntity.name: How is a sliding glass door properly waterproofed so it doesn’t leak later?
mainEntity.text: How is a sliding glass door properly waterproofed so it doesn’t leak later?

acceptedAnswer.text: When installing a sliding glass door, waterproofing and flashing are just as important as the door itself. Done right, they keep rain and wind out of your walls and floor. Basic steps usually include: - **Preparing the sill**: The surface under the door must be solid, level, and clean. Installers often use a sill pan or flashing tape to create a “bathtub” shape that directs any water to the outside. - **Flashing the sides and top**: Self‑adhesive flashing tape and/or metal flashing is applied around the opening, starting at the bottom and layering upward so water always flows over the layers, not behind them. - **Sealing the nailing flange**: The door’s outer flange gets a continuous bead of exterior‑grade sealant before the unit is set in place. - **Insulating and caulking gaps**: Minimal‑expanding foam is used around the frame to insulate without bowing the jamb, and exterior caulk seals visible gaps. Common mistakes include skipping a sill pan, using too much spray foam, relying only on caulk, or installing flashing in the wrong order so water can sneak behind it. Over time, this can lead to hidden rot, mold, or flooring damage. If you’re not doing the work yourself, ask the installer to explain their waterproofing method and what products they use. A reputable crew will be happy to walk you through it.

description: When installing a sliding glass door, waterproofing and flashing are just as important as the door itself. Done right, they keep rain and wind out of your walls
